As others allude to, this isn’t great. Transitions frome one page to another are slow and too animated. They don’t load at once, so you go to tap something and it jumps down a few pegs because something else has loaded. Links, help pages, and redirects end you up in circles. e.g. ‘track a fault’ opens in a browser and an endless loading screen). A really poor app from what claims to be the most reliable network in the UK.

I use it for managing full-fibre 500mbps broadband. I would estimate 1 in 3 speed tests through the app result in it getting stuck on ‘almost there’, and having to force close the app a few times. Sometimes, it will show a hub speed of 500mbps+ and device speed of 50mps and say ‘great speeds’ - it’s not great though is it? 

Pressing ‘manage broadband’ takes me to another page where I then have to presss ‘Broadband’ again. The broadband page takes a second or two to load, and does so in stages. If you come out the app and back in(e.g. to answer a message), the page reloads and you get the dreaded loading circles again. Press ‘service status’ and you get a blank screen while it checks ‘something’. Following links like ‘track fault’ open broswers and you get endless loading or errors. You then have to use the browser navigation to go back to ‘track fault’ and it opens - which defeats the point of the app? When searching, the results default to ‘Shop’ instead of ‘Help’. Why would I want to search ‘network issues’ and be directed to the shop?

When you do a speed test and you’re getting less than your guaranteed minimum speed, it automatically opens a fault log with EE. This is great, but the app doesn’t tell you it’s opened a fault. You have to go looking for it, and even then the in-app links don’t work. The AI messaging function can also be pretty unhelpful despite clear prompts. 

There are other aspects of the app which work really well and are easy to follow, but the transition speed and animations ruin the user experience. That and the whole ‘links opening in broswer’ thing defeats the point of having an app in the first place, as I could have just used my browser and saved time. Particularly when you have to log-in again when directed away from the app.

Fair app, but I and many other had problems setting up two-factor authentication (2FA).  That is because the app doesn’t handle Apple’s format for numbers (‘+44 XXXX XXXXXX’).  On has to manually enter a different format (‘0XXXX XXXXXX’) to set up 2FA.  

Kinda amateurish that the app doesn’t translate the Apple format for phone numbers into what the app requires.  An unpaid undergraduate computer science intern could probably do that in less than a day….

Oh, and the developer response seems to be from an AI chatbot which suggests that I contact EE help at their web site and report the bug.  A human responding to this review would have done that themselves.  Again, amateurish.

At first, I was unsure of dealing with my bills and things on a mobile app as I’ve always likes to call up and deal with a real person when it comes to money matters but I was convinced to download MY O2 and it has brought a bit of modern magic into my life! The little bill reminders have really come in handy as well, I am a busy bee so keeping track of when those are due can be a right pain sometimes, a little nudge from my phone has been my saving grace on more than one occasion! I am still not as tech-savvy as I am sure most people using this app are, but I have had no problems and reckon if I can use it anyone can! It is all nicely laid out for me on the screen, and they have not overcrowded it with useless information which so many of these apps. If you do get stuck there are lots of ways to get help either with a person, a robot, or the FAQ page so the option is quite nice. Big thumbs up from me, having an app keeping an eye on my bills and stuff has been a bit of a game changer!

When I enquired about roaming in France at an EE shop (having been on EE for some years) I was told I can’t get roaming till I land at my destination, when I will get a text. So a couple of weeks ago I went to France. I did get a text. I did reply to the text. Did I get roaming? … no. The link? Well I’m damned if I know what happened to THAT. So .. I was in France and needing that roaming, trying to use the app, trying every which way I could to buy a roaming package for 12 days. I had to retire to a room to try and try. It took a couple of fruitless hours. Partly because the app wouldn’t co-operate till I’d updated it, but I couldn’t update it because there was no wifi and I NEEDED roaming just to communicate with the people I was meeting at Le Mans 24 hr race. Eventually I got what I assume was a chat bot. Do you want to buy the roaming it says after I’d said I wanted to buy roaming. So I thought I’d got there finally. I even got a message confirming the £27 odd roaming fee. Did I get roaming? No idea. I’m glad I took a screenshot of that message because it disappeared from EVERYTHING immediately. I wondered .. maybe it’s because I have a £20 cap on my account? Tried to increase the cap - couldn’t. Couldn’t get chat back. Couldn’t check my account to see if you’ve charged £27. Still can’t. I can’t see what I’ve spent in France, not at all. Keep in control??  You must be joking! My husband gave up with his (also EE)  and he never got the text when we ‘landed’, my son in law had the same problem as I did and back in the UK rang you up to see what’s going on and what his charges are “sorry we can’t access your bill till the charging date”. 
In this day and age such a well known company SHOULD be able to sell roaming before you go to the EU at least. Other networks do!!! You should be more responsive - you always were!!! Your app should do what it says it does. Unless I want to spend more money with you it does NOT. I’m caught in a signal trap. No other network except 3 works in this area of rural Wales. 
And PLEASE don’t give me your cut and paste stock answer about valuing feedback and to contact you to discuss the matter. I don’t want to spend even more of my time going round in circles of frustration. 
And if services depend on an updated app then for goodness sake update it automatically because the damn thing won’t update unless you’re on wifi and wifi isn’t always available when you’ve just landed in France! and it seems when you use the app to do anything meaningful you get a message to say turn your wifi off. Well without wifi in my house there IS NO SIGNAL!

It’s okay. Better than most but could be improved on stuff like trending usage and insights, to actually help you use less energy … 

the insights shown are completely meaningless e.g. comparing day on day usage and saying “usage higher/lower than usual” … my yesterday usage was slighter than the day before and it thus tells you you’re using “more than usual”, even though I’m actually using about 50% less than the average I’ve used for six months. 

Surely can do better on insights than just comparing it with the previous day?! Someone with the most basic secondary school level maths could produce better insights using mental arithmetic than this app, just by looking at the graphs/trend. 

Between this app and the useless in-house monitor they issue (the monitor I had for my first generation meters in 2017 was better), you’re better off just downloading a load of usage data from their site, shoving it through excel and then letting it produce trends and analysis insights for you. Or uploading it to chat gpt and letting it do the hard work for you..

In this day and age of automation of data analysis, you’d expect Octopus to be investing more in a better app.

In addition, the balance forecast and automatic changing of your monthly amount - although you can override it - is infuriating. Seems to base it on most recent usage then extrapolates it to future rather than looking at your LONG TERM USAGE AND LONG TERM TRENDS. so you can end up paying more than you should because it reforecasts it based on recency/ blips etc which take no account of things like the weather… Instead of being intelligent and really using the huge amount of data they hold on you to produce sophisticated and realistic balance forecasts. 

Also.. used to be better also re screen refresh: Now every time you switch temporarily to a different app, when you come back to Octopus app it seems to have to reload/refresh the content … infuriating. Didn’t do this previously. Best sticking with web site imo.

I have been an O2 customer for over ten years, and it is genuinely disappointing to see how far the company has declined since its partnership with Virgin Media.

Despite paying increasingly higher monthly costs, the mobile signal remains poor. It is difficult to justify rising prices when the quality of the service has failed to improve.

The O2 app is equally frustrating. It serves little practical purpose when making a payment. After using Face ID to log into the app, I am then redirected to the Virgin Media O2 website, where I have to authenticate my account multiple times before I can finally pay my bill. If customers are simply going to be redirected to a website, what is the point of the app in the first place? It feels unnecessarily complicated and poorly designed.

Unfortunately, customer service also appears to have deteriorated and now resembles the standard that Virgin Media has long been criticised for. The level of service I once associated with O2 is no longer there.

With just one month remaining on my contract, I have made the decision not to renew. After a decade of loyalty, I will be taking my business elsewhere.

I recently contacted British Gas because I wanted to switch to a cheaper electricity tariff. What I found frustrating is that the cheapest rates are only available if you agree to a Direct Debit arrangement based on estimated usage.

My issue is not with paying my bills. I have no problem paying for the energy I use. The issue is that I would prefer my payments to reflect my actual consumption rather than an estimated amount chosen by the supplier.

If I want more control over my payments and prefer to wait for the bill before paying the exact amount owed, I am charged a higher tariff. If I choose Direct Debit to access the cheaper tariff, I am expected to pay an estimated monthly amount that may be higher than my actual usage, potentially building up credit on the account.

To me, this feels unfair. Customers who simply want to pay the correct amount for what they actually use should not be financially penalised for choosing that option. I don’t want to avoid paying my bills; I just want to pay the right amount at the right time.

Unfortunately, when I contacted customer support, I did not receive a clear explanation or any meaningful help regarding this concern. The response was simply that Direct Debit was the cheaper option, which did not address the issue I was raising.

British Gas should offer customers more flexibility and transparency when it comes to payment methods, without effectively charging extra for those who prefer to pay their actual bill rather than an estimate.

⭐ 1 Star – Absolutely Terrible Customer Service

This has been one of the most frustrating customer service experiences I have ever had.I would give zero stars if I could, not less then a nightmare. 

I spent more than 8 hours just trying to cancel a Vodafone plan that I had only just taken out, and the process was an absolute nightmare. I was transferred from agent to agent repeatedly, and every single person seemed to tell me something completely different. I called multiple times and was constantly passed around different departments instead of getting a clear answer or solution.

One agent was particularly rude and started making excuses before even understanding my situation. He immediately claimed that I had obtained the plan through a third party without even checking my account or listening to my problem. Instead of helping, he was simply looking for reasons not to deal with the issue.

What made the experience even worse is that Vodafone does not provide a straightforward option to cancel a plan online or through the app. You are forced to speak to agents, and unfortunately many of them seem more interested in passing the problem to someone else than actually resolving it. Every conversation ended with, "I'll transfer you to another team," only for the next person to say the same thing. Nobody seemed to take ownership of the issue.

I was originally told the plan would cost £8, but I was then charged £16. These additional charges were never clearly explained to me beforehand, which left me feeling misled and frustrated. Transparency should be a basic part of customer service.

What I find most disappointing is the contrast between the sales process and the cancellation process. When you're signing up, everything is quick, easy, and friendly. But the moment you want to cancel or make changes, it suddenly becomes incredibly difficult. It should not take hours of phone calls, multiple transfers, and endless explanations just to leave a service.

I have also experienced similar issues with Vodafone broadband, where even making changes or cancelling services becomes unnecessarily complicated.

My advice to anyone considering Vodafone is to read every part of the contract very carefully before signing up. If you ever need support, be prepared for long waits, conflicting information, and a lot of time wasted. Abs 

Overall, a very disappointing experience and one that has seriously damaged my trust in Vodafone.
